返回本批次

为什么没有人意识到我们要重返月球？

Why Doesn't Anybody Realize We're Going Back to the Moon?

www.theatlantic.com 作者 paulpauper 分数 63 评论 109 2026/04/03 06:32
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 自阿波罗时代以来最重大的一次发射即将开始，沿着佛罗里达州的太空海岸，聚集的人群中弥漫着一种二手的兴奋感，仿佛我们所有人，而不仅仅是宇航员，很快就会摆脱地球引力，骑在火柱上。黎明前，在满月的黄色月光下，太空爱好者们开始抵达泰特斯维尔的 A. Max Brewer 大桥。他们在桥的最高点支起了折叠椅和三脚架，以获得最佳视线，并在短暂的下雨期间固定在原地，随后，当一阵令人担忧的风吹过泻湖时，他们再次固定在原地。在发射台上，火箭矗立在 30 多层楼高的地方，被体育场灯光照亮。随着时间的流逝，桥下的公园里弥漫着一种尾随的气氛。绵延数英里的时间里，几乎所有可用的草地上都停满了汽车。一家人都铺着野餐毯，大学生们推着冷藏箱。他们来回扔足球和棒球，喝啤酒。日落前两个小时，我们数千人聚集在桥上。人们一次又一次告诉我，他们是来看历史的。如果阿耳忒弥斯二号成功，宇航员将成为 50 多年来第一批抵达月球轨道的人类，他们绕月球背面的路径将带他们进入宇宙，比任何人类之前走过的更远。我旁边的那个人正在用手机观看美国宇航局的电视转播。他告诉我，我们距离升空只有一分钟了。阿耳忒弥斯二号任务似乎突然出现。一月份，在早期尝试发布的几周前，我开始询问朋友是否期待它。他们中很少有人知道这件事正在发生。 “我们要回月球吗？”他们会问，带着一种轻微的惊讶，就像人们被告知距离超级碗比赛只剩下一周了一样。他们没有在这个话题上停留。不管怎样，孩子们怎么样？ 20 世纪 60 年代最初的登月任务可以作为历史上最伟大的冒险来出售。阿波罗计划是美国一个世纪技术变革的三重感叹号，在此期间，美国人实现了城市电气化，街道上充满了汽车，天空中充满了飞机，分裂了原子，并发明了数字计算机。为了完成这一向未来的转变，国家联合起来建造了一艘载着人类前往另一个世界的宇宙飞船，并进行了千古插旗，一个和平的硫磺岛。阿波罗计划的（前纳粹和）知识分子建筑师沃纳·冯·布劳恩将登月与水生生物首次爬上陆地的划时代时刻进行了比较。 在此后的几十年里，阿波罗计划失去了一些光环，部分原因是它没有像包括冯·布劳恩本人在内的建筑师所希望的那样，带来辉煌的太空未来。六十年过去了，在此期间我们发明了互联网、智能手机和强大的人工智能，但今年的发射不是针对火星、外行星或恒星。我们刚刚返回月球。尽管世界上四分之三的人口还很年轻，从未经历过像这样的载人登月任务，但它给人一种重演的感觉。我们再次赶在另一个国家之前到达，这次是中国而不是苏联。我们被告知，如果我们不先到达那里，中国人可能会拥有最好的水冰陨石坑。 Gerardo Mora / Getty 数千名观众聚集在佛罗里达州泰特斯维尔的一座桥上，观看半个世纪以来的第一次登月发射。美国宇航局局长贾里德·艾萨克曼经常说，阿尔忒弥斯计划将与阿波罗计划不同，因为这次我们要去月球停留。但我们真的是这样吗？阿耳忒弥斯二号宇航员甚至不会离开他们的飞船。他们甚至不会在月球上停留。当他们绕过它的另一边后，他们就会回家。美国宇航局已经制定了未来阿耳忒弥斯登月计划，据称宇航员将为核动力月球基地奠定基础。不过，尽管这些计划不仅仅是 PowerPoint 幻灯片上的效果图，但它们可能需要经受住国会中几次 NASA 预算之争以及至少一次总统换届的考验。阿波罗计划的建筑师也有关于月球基地的宏伟计划，但如果没有持续的政治支持，这些计划就会落空。在桥上，卡尔·乌尔茨海默（Carl Ulzheimer），一位自称“来自布朗克斯的老狗”，告诉我，他一定要来参加这次登月发射，因为可能要过 50 年才能再举行一次，而他没有那样的时间。这次阿耳忒弥斯发射是在美国 250 岁生日前夕进行的，这更加让人感觉这是对婴儿潮一代老年统治的怀旧行为。更重要的是，因为唐纳德·特朗普，有史以来最年长的入主白宫的人，正在主持整个事件。他的政府试图破坏美国宇航局的科学任务，但总统似乎很高兴让该机构代表他掀起一场全国性的奇观，就像他很高兴在自己的生日那天举行阅兵式一样。对我来说，他没有在火箭上印上自己的脸，或者以其他方式将自己置于发射的中心，这算是一个小小的怜悯。住在附近的布拉德·科瓦尔斯基告诉我，特朗普至少应该下来看看。 “这狗娘养的不在这里，这很重要，”他说。从统计数据来看，肯定有很多总统的粉丝在桥上，但我很惊讶没有看到一顶红色的 MAGA 帽子。阅读：唐纳德·特朗普如何试图让美国宇航局的科学任务落地世界为美国之前的宇宙荣耀时刻欢呼。在一次阿波罗任务之后，一位苏联太空科学家祝贺美国标志着“地球人普世文化发展的新阶段”。但随着特朗普在发射前 30 天发动战争和全球能源危机，地球人普世文化的想法突然变得古怪而遥远。 距离发射还有 30 秒，我凝视着泻湖对面，被火箭迷住了。太空发射系统多年来一直是一个无用的技术。从每次发射成本来看，它可能是有史以来最昂贵的火箭。最初的版本花了 11 年的时间来开发，而土星五号只花了 6 年的时间，而且它的发布节奏慢得可怕。最糟糕的是，作为新的可重新发射技术时代的一次性火箭，SLS 已经过时了，而且在阿耳忒弥斯任务真正开始登陆月球之前，该设计很可能会停止。然而，多么漂亮的火箭啊！在发射台上，它具有复古未来主义的魅力，尤其是因为其构造中使用了旧的航天飞机部件。大型核心级采用与将航天飞机送入轨道的大型核心级相同的独特橙色泡沫进行绝缘。在它的两侧，有两个细长的白色助推器，上面用古老的“蠕虫”字体写着 NASA 字样，使每个字母的锐角变得圆润，并将 As 简化为颠倒的 V。一旦这些助推器中的可怕炸药开始燃烧，它们就无法被阻止。还没有人类乘坐过这个系统，然而在其顶部的小型圆锥形太空舱中，四名宇航员向后倾斜在座位上，直视上方，等待点火。Austin DeSisto / NurPhoto / Getty 与最初的登月不同，阿耳忒弥斯二号宇航员甚至不会离开他们的飞船。他们甚至不会在月球上停留。当他们绕过它的远端后，他们就会回家。桥上的人群刚刚结束倒计时，火箭毗邻塔的手臂就拉开了。美国宇航局称这个过程为脐带分离。起初，发布会本身就像一部无声小电影。火箭底座冒出滚滚浓烟，随后燃起第一道火焰。然后，一条你所见过的最亮的橙色光芒的翻腾河流径直射向垫子。一时间，周围的大部分沼泽看起来都被吞没了。第二个太阳似乎已经升起，整个巨大的火箭奇迹般地从太阳中升起。孩子们站在我周围，张着嘴，当声音最终响起时，他们瑟缩了。那是神圣的滚滚雷霆。当我们看着宇宙飞船在天空中升起时，它震动了桥梁并笼罩了我们的整个身体。就在那时，我们感觉自己与宇航员是多么融洽。美国宇航局与其说选择了其机组人员，不如说选择了他们的演员，在这个黑暗的时刻，他们尽最大努力体现了健康的美国意识。该任务的指挥官里德·怀斯曼（Reid Wiseman）是一位有两个女儿的单亲父亲，他的妻子（一名儿科护士）因癌症去世。飞行员维克多·格洛弗 (Victor Glover) 在南加州的内陆帝国长大。在成为宇航员之前，他去了海军驾驶战斗机。现在他是第一个登上月球的黑人。坐在他旁边的是克里斯蒂娜·科赫(Christina Koch)，她是多次南极探险的资深人士，也曾在国际空间站呆了近一年，在此期间，她参加了首次全女性太空行走。在这次任务中，她将再次创造历史，机组人员中唯一的加拿大人杰里米·汉森也将成为第一个超越近地轨道的非美国人。他们四人现在正在向平流层疾驰。仅仅两分钟后，助推器就失效了，但我们仍然可以看到飞船尾随的火焰比它本身还要长。宇航员还没有飞到半空中，飞行控制权就已经转移到美国宇航局位于休斯敦的载人航天神经中枢。在地面控制中，成排的技术人员会引导他们绕地球两圈，然后发射最后一次燃烧来拦截月球，月球本身的移动速度超过每小时 2,200 英里。当宇航员看着我们的蓝色星球从他们的视野中退去时，他们会想什么？亚克力水族箱玻璃窗户？世界事务可能会呈现出与上面不同的面貌。在人类首次登陆月球、即阿波罗 8 号飞越月球任务之前，美国人也发现自己国家的文化四分五裂。春节攻势表明，越南战争可能会演变成一场更长、更血腥的战争。马丁·路德·金和鲍比·肯尼迪刚刚被暗杀，民主党全国代表大会陷入骚乱。据说阿波罗八号为国家治愈提供了一些措施，但这些东西很难衡量。圣诞节前夕，宇航员们来到月球背​​面时，轮流朗读《创世记》：起初，神创造了天地，地是空虚混沌；起初，神创造了天地，地是空虚混沌；神创造了天地，创造了天地。深渊的表面一片黑暗。他们用相机试图捕捉我们家乡星球的辉煌。机组人员拍摄的地球从灰暗的月球平原升起的快照被认为为我们提供了新的宇宙方向，并激发了环保运动。但同样，这些事情很难衡量。我们是否敢希望阿耳忒弥斯二号能够带来类似的历史庄严时刻？宇航员肯定会发回一些非凡的图像。这次任务的时间安排是让太阳在他们的背后。地质学家指导他们从船上观察远处的某些特征，包括东方海，这是一个撞击坑，里面有同心环的山脉，终止于一个黑色的熔岩盆地，即靶心。没有人亲眼目睹过它的原地。在 30 到 50 分钟内，宇航员将与 NASA 失去联系，他们将独自欣赏这些新鲜的景色。当他们再次看到地球时，地球将与月球相形见绌。也许宇航员会被感动，对它的脆弱说出一些新的、美丽的话；也许所有这些话都已经说过了。不管怎样，他们已经回家了。第七天，他们的正式日程要求休息。但这一切都是未来的事情了。又过了一会儿，阿耳忒弥斯号飞船仍然属于地球。当它从视野中消失后，我环顾四周，看到人们拥抱在一起，用世俗语言说话，发出巨大的欢呼声，高兴地大笑。一些大学生开始高喊“美国！美国！”他们看起来可能会冲进一个足球场并撞倒一些立柱。火箭令人敬畏的声音最终逐渐减弱为微弱的隆隆声。我们再次听到下面海浪拍打的声音。东方，地平线上的某个地方，满月即将升起。

Original Summary

  1. The most momentous launch since the Apollo era was about to begin, and along Florida’s space coast, a secondhand exhilaration was working its way through the assembled crowd, as though all of us, and not just the astronauts, would soon ride out of Earth’s gravity well on a pillar of fire. The space faithful had started arriving at the A. Max Brewer Bridge in Titusville before dawn, under the light of a full, yellow moon. They had set up their folding chairs and tripods at the high point of the bridge, to get the best line of sight, and stayed fixed in place during a brief rain, and again later, when a concerning wind blew across the lagoon.On the launchpad, the rocket stood more than 30 stories high, illuminated by banks of stadium lights. As the day wore on, a tailgate atmosphere took hold in the park at the foot of the bridge. For miles, cars had pulled onto nearly every available stretch of grass. Families spread out on picnic blankets, and college-age kids wheeled out coolers. They threw footballs and baseballs back and forth and drank beer.Two hours before sunset, thousands of us massed on the bridge. Again and again, people told me that they’d come to see history. If Artemis II is successful, the astronauts will be the first humans to reach the moon’s orbit in more than 50 years, and their path around its far side will take them farther into the universe than any human being has previously traveled. The man next to me was streaming the NASA telecast on his phone. He told me that we were one minute from liftoff.The Artemis II mission has seemingly come out of nowhere. In January, a few weeks before an earlier launch attempt, I’d started asking friends if they were looking forward to it. Few of them had any idea it was happening. “We’re going back to the moon?” they would ask, with the sort of mild surprise that one might experience upon being told that the Super Bowl is only a week away. They didn’t linger on the subject. Anyway, how are the kids?The original moon missions of the 1960s could be sold as history’s grandest adventure. The Apollo program was the triple-back-handspring exclamation mark on a century of American technological transformations, during which Americans had electrified their cities, filled their streets with cars and their skies with airplanes, split atoms, and invented digital computers. To complete this phase shift into the future, the country banded together to build a spaceship that carried humans to another world, and performed a flag planting for the ages, a peaceful Iwo Jima. Wernher von Braun, the (ex-Nazi and) intellectual architect of Apollo, had compared the moon landing to the epochal moment when aquatic life had first crawled onto land.In the decades since, the Apollo program has lost some of its aura, in part because it did not lead to a glorious space future in the way that its architects, including von Braun himself, had hoped. Six decades have passed, during which time we’ve had the invention of the internet, the smartphone, and powerful artificial intelligence, and yet this year’s launch is not to Mars or the outer planets or the stars. We’re just returning to the moon. Even though three-fourths of the world’s population is young enough to have never experienced a crewed lunar mission like this one, it has the feel of a rerun. Again we’re rushing to arrive before another nation does, this time China instead of the Soviet Union. We’re told that if we don’t get there first, the Chinese might claim the craters with the best water ice.Gerardo Mora / Getty Thousands of spectators gathered on a bridge in Titusville, Florida, to watch the first moon launch in half a century.Jared Isaacman, NASA’s administrator, often says that the Artemis program will be different from Apollo because this time we’re going to the moon to stay. But are we really? The Artemis II astronauts won’t even leave their ship. They won’t even be stopping at the moon. After they swing around its far side, they’ll come right home. NASA has plans for future Artemis moon landings, in which astronauts will supposedly lay the foundations for a nuclear-powered moon base. But although these plans are more than mere renderings on a PowerPoint slide, they will likely need to weather several NASA budget fights in Congress and at least one presidential transition. The Apollo program’s architects had grand plans for a moon base, too, but without sustained political support, those came to nothing. On the bridge, Carl Ulzheimer, a self-described “old dog from the Bronx,” told me that he’d made sure to come to this moon launch, because 50 years might pass before there was another one, and he didn’t have that kind of time.That this Artemis launch is happening in the lead-up to America’s 250th birthday has heightened the sense that it’s a nostalgia act for the Baby Boomer gerontocracy. All the more so because Donald Trump, the oldest person ever to be elected to the White House, is presiding over the whole affair. His administration has sought to sabotage NASA’s scientific missions, but the president seems delighted to have the agency gin up a national spectacle on his behalf, just as he was happy to have a military parade on his birthday.To me, it was a small mercy that he hadn’t embossed his own face on the rocket, or otherwise put himself at the center of the launch. Brad Kowalski, who lives nearby, told me that Trump should have at least come down to see it. “It’s significant that the son of a bitch isn’t here,” he said. Just as a matter of statistics, surely many of the president’s fans were on the bridge, but I was surprised not to see a single red MAGA cap.Read: How Donald Trump tried to ground NASA’s science missionsThe world has cheered on America’s previous moments of cosmic glory. After one Apollo mission, a Soviet space scientist congratulated the United States for marking a new “stage in the development of the universal culture of Earthmen.” But with Trump having started a war and a global energy crisis in the 30-day run-up to launch, the idea of a universal culture of Earthmen suddenly felt quaint and distant.With 30 seconds to go, I stared across the lagoon, mesmerized by the rocket. The Space Launch System is a techno-boondoggle for the ages. On a cost-per-launch basis, it’s likely the most expensive rocket ever built. The initial version took 11 years to develop—the Saturn V took just six—and its launch cadence has been dreadfully slow. Worst of all, as a single-use rocket in the age of new, relaunchable technology, SLS is already obsolete, and the design may well be discontinued before the Artemis missions actually start landing on the moon.And yet, what a handsome rocket! On the launchpad, it had a retro-futuristic charisma, not least because old space-shuttle parts were used in its construction. The large core stage is insulated in the same distinctive orange foam as the big one that fired the space shuttle into orbit. On its sides, two slim, white boosters have NASA written on them in that old “worm” font that rounds off every letter’s sharp angles, and simplifies the As into upside-down Vs. Once the monstrous explosives in these boosters begin to burn, they can’t be stopped. No human has ever caught a ride on this system, and yet in the small, conical capsule at its top, the four astronauts were tilted back in seats, staring straight up, awaiting ignition.Austin DeSisto / NurPhoto / Getty Unlike with the original moon landing, the Artemis II astronauts won’t even leave their ship. They won’t even be stopping at the moon. After they swing around its far side, they’ll come right home.The crowd on the bridge had just finished counting down when the arms of the rocket’s adjoining tower pulled away. NASA calls this process umbilical separation. At first, the launch itself was like a little silent movie. Smoke billowed out from the rocket’s base, followed by the first flames. And then, a churning river of the brightest orange light you have ever seen shot straight down at the pad. For a moment, much of the surrounding marsh looked to be engulfed. A second sun seemed to have risen, and the whole hulking rocket was ascending out of it, miraculously. Children stood around me, slack-jawed, and when the sound finally hit, they flinched. It was a holy rolling thunder. It shook the bridge and enveloped our entire bodies as we watched the spaceship rise and rise in the sky.How joined we felt to the astronauts, just then. NASA doesn’t so much choose its crews as cast them, and in this dark moment, they’d done their best to embody a wholesome sense of America. The mission’s commander, Reid Wiseman, is a single dad of two daughters who lost his wife, a pediatric nurse, to cancer. Victor Glover, its pilot, grew up in Southern California’s Inland Empire. Before he became an astronaut, he went to the Navy to fly fighter jets. Now he’s the first Black man ever to journey to the moon. Sitting alongside him is Christina Koch, a veteran of several Antarctic expeditions, who also once spent nearly a year at the International Space Station, during which she participated in the first all-female space walks. On this mission, she’ll make history again, as will the crew’s lone Canadian, Jeremy Hansen, when he becomes the first non-American to venture beyond low Earth orbit.The four of them were now streaking toward the stratosphere. After only two minutes, the boosters fell away, exhausted, but we could still see the spaceship trailed by a flame longer than itself. The astronauts had not even gone halfway up into the dome of the sky, and control of the flight had already shifted to NASA’s nerve center for human spaceflight, in Houston. In ground control, the rows of technicians would guide them as they twice circled the Earth, and then fired one last burn to intercept the moon, which is itself moving at more than 2,200 miles an hour.What will the astronauts be thinking, as they watch our blue planet receding through their windows of acrylic aquarium glass? The world’s affairs may take on a different cast from up there. Before humanity’s first visit to the moon, the lunar-flyby mission of Apollo 8, Americans had also found the culture of their country riven. The Tet Offensive suggested that the Vietnam War could spiral into something longer and bloodier. Martin Luther King Jr. and Bobby Kennedy had just been assassinated, and the Democratic National Convention had descended into riots.Apollo 8 is said to have provided some measure of national healing, but these things are difficult to measure. On Christmas Eve, as the astronauts came around the moon’s far side, they took turns reading from the book of Genesis: In the beginning, God created the heaven and the earth, and the earth was without form, and void; and darkness was upon the face of the deep. With cameras, they tried to capture the glory of our home planet. A snapshot that the crew took of Earth rising from the ashen lunar plain has been credited with giving us a new cosmic orientation, and galvanizing the environmental movement. But again, these things are hard to measure.Do we even dare to hope that Artemis II might deliver a similar moment of historical gravitas? The astronauts will surely send back some extraordinary images. The mission has been timed so that the sun will be at their backs. Geologists have coached them to look out from their ship at certain features of the far side, including the Mare Orientale, an impact crater with concentric rings of mountains inside it, which terminate in a dark lava basin, a bullseye. No human has ever laid eyes on it, in situ. For 30 to 50 minutes, the astronauts will lose communication with NASA, and they’ll be alone with these fresh vistas. When they see the Earth again, it will be dwarfed by the moon. Perhaps the astronauts will be moved to say something new and beautiful about its fragility; perhaps all such things have already been said. Either way, they’ll already be homebound. On the seventh day, their official schedule calls for rest.But all that is in the future. For just another few moments, the Artemis spaceship still belonged to the Earth. After it faded from view, I looked around and saw people hugging, speaking in secular tongues, making great whooping noises, laughing with glee. A few college boys started chanting “USA! USA!” They looked like they might storm a football field and knock down some uprights. The awesome sound of the rocket finally tapered to a faint rumble. We could once again hear waves lapping below. To the east, somewhere on the horizon, the full moon was about to rise.

评论（默认中文）

oconnor663 2026/04/03 07:15
我对“到月球”这个词的选择提出质疑。我明白了，这在技术上是正确的，但是大约 100% 的普通人听到这句话并认为这意味着脚踏实地。每次提到这个问题，都会立即做出让观众失望的澄清。这不是一个自信的节目会做出的营销选择。
I question the choice of the phrase "to the Moon". I get it, it's technically true, but ~100% of normal people hear that and assume it means boots on the ground. Every single time it gets mentioned, it's immediately followed by a clarification that disappoints the audience. This isn't the sort of marketing choice that a self-confident program makes.

trashface 2026/04/03 13:34
曾经喜欢太空的东西，尽管其中一些很可怕（年龄足以记得小时候在电视上看过挑战者爆炸）。 不太理解这个月球努力。 太空对人类来说是独一无二的敌意，在我看来，我们的技术还没有准备好，而且可能在 100 年内都不会准备好。 我们的角色当然还没有准备好——我比大多数人能忍受更多的孤立，我经常一两周没有任何真正的人际接触，通常几天不与任何人说话。 然而，生活在月球或火星殖民地的想法让我对它所涉及的孤立感到严重犹豫。 空间站有一个不同的问题，因为我想在几周后将人们推出气闸室，或者以这种方式弹出自己。

无论如何，这次月球旅行似乎毫无意义。 我可以理解为什么其他国家想要登陆那里，但对于美国来说，月球探险的价值为零，特别是当我们在愚蠢的战争上烧了这么多钱，同时削减对公民的医疗保健和食品支持时。

Used to love space stuff, although some of it was horrible (old enough to remember watching the challenger blow up on TV as a pre-teen). Don't really understand this moon effort. Space is uniquely hostile to humans, our tech just isn't ready IMO and probably won't be for 100 years. Our personas certainly aren't ready - I can tolerate far more isolation than most people, I often go 1-2 weeks without any real human contact at all, routinely go days without speaking to anyone. And yet the idea of living on a moon or mars colony gives me serious pause for the isolation it would involve. Space station has a different problem in that i'd be wanting to push people out of the airlock after a few weeks, or eject myself that way.

Anyway this moon trip seems totally pointless. I can get why other countries want to land there but for the US, moon expeditions have zero value, especially when we are burning so much money on stupid wars and and the same time cutting healthcare and food support for citizens.

在 HN 中查看
cagey 2026/04/03 10:52
> 因此，我们将在 60 年后重建阿波罗 8 号任务。

甚至不是：阿波罗 8[0] 进入绕月轨道（绕月 10 圈），然后离开月球轨道返回地球。 这需要关键任务火箭燃烧以进入（LOI）和退出（TEI）月球轨道。 阿耳忒弥斯 II[1] 只是进行一次“飞越”；它永远不会进入月球轨道，这是一项挑战性/风险较小的任务。

[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apollo_8#Lunar_orbit

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artemis_II#Lunar_flyby

> So we're re-creating the Apollo 8 Mission 60 years later.

Not even: Apollo 8[0] went into orbit around the moon (orbited 10 times), then left lunar orbit to return to Earth. This required mission-critical rocket burns both to enter (LOI) and exit (TEI) lunar orbit. Artemis II[1] is merely doing a "fly-by"; it'll never enter lunar orbit, a much less challenging/risky mission.

[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apollo_8#Lunar_orbit

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artemis_II#Lunar_flyby

在 HN 中查看
palata 2026/04/03 08:08
> 并有可能实现更多、更有趣的任务。

作为人类，我们走得越远就是火星，它也没用。下一颗恒星是如此如此遥远，即使考虑用“某种东西”到达它也需要基础物理学的一场革命。我想，不需要为此建造火箭，只需一块白板和物理学家即可。

说我们去火星是 极其 慷慨的。前往那里的火箭工程很有趣，但如果你想把人类送到那里，他们必须在旅途中幸存下来。例如，包括旅行期间的饮食和呼吸空气。这些都不是解决的问题。很有可能，在我们将人类送上火星之前，我们的社会就已经崩溃了。

> and potentially enable further, more interesting missions.

The further we go as humans is Mars, and it's useless. The next star is so, so, so far away that even considering reaching it with "something" requires a revolution in fundamental physics. No need to build rockets for that, just a whiteboard and physicists, I guess.

And saying that we go to Mars is extremely generous. The engineering of the rocket going there is fun, but if you want to send humans there, they have to survive the trip. Including, for instance, eating and drinking and breathing air for the duration of the trip. Those are not solved problems. Chances are that we as a society collapse long before we get to send humans to Mars.

skeeter2020 2026/04/03 08:01
我只是对我们能学到的东西不太感兴趣。我可以去一次长途旅行，在我的小型货车里吃饭、睡觉（但可能不能拉屎）；关于搬到一个新的城市或国家，这教会了我什么？我可以开车穿越整个国家，绕休斯敦的环路转一圈；这并没有告诉我那里的生活是什么样的。

我们本可以派出没有宇航员的飞船来测试所有系统并了解唯一真正有价值的问题：这东西有用吗？相反，我们得到的是戏剧和政治，以及更昂贵的任务。

I'm just not that jazzed on what we could possibly learn. I can go on a big road trip and eat, sleep (but probably not poop) in my minivan; what does that teach me about moving to a new city or country? I can drive across the country and do a loop around Houston's ring road; that tells me nothing about what it's like to live there.

We could have sent the ship without astronauts to test all the systems and learn the only real valuable question: does this thing work? Instead we get drama & politics, and a much more expensive mission.

在 HN 中查看
throw0101c 2026/04/03 07:54
> 绕月飞行 60 年后，我们将再次尝试这一壮举。我很难兴奋起来...

几年前，有一位喜剧演员观察到，我们已经失去了敬畏和惊奇的眼光：当互联网刚刚引入时，他在飞机上，并在飞机上宣布了这一点，但过了一会儿它就停止工作了，他们宣布“技术困难”，无法使用。

旁边的那个人就像是“这是胡说八道“：世界多么快就欠了这个家伙几分钟前才知道的东西。

他继续说道：每当人们抱怨他们的航班时，它就像一辆 20 世纪 40 年代的德国运牛车：X 发生了，然后 Y 发生了。他的回答是：然后发生了什么？ 你在空中飞过吗？ 你坐在椅子上吗 在天空中？ 像鸟一样，像人类一样 自伊卡洛斯尾巴（及之前）以来的成像？

享乐适应是真实的（就其本身而言，这“很好”，因为追求更好并不是一件坏事）：

* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hedonic_treadmill

但考虑到你正在援引历史，人们很容易忘记阿波罗 8 号之前几十年人类所经历的苦难，以及所发生的令人难以置信的进步（即使是现在，地球上的许多人还没有完全经历过）：

* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Rise_and_Fall_of_American_...

> 60 years after swinging around the moon, we are going to attempt the feat again. I'm having a hard time getting excited...

There was a comedian that had the observation a few years back that we've lost our saw of awe and wonder: he was on a plane when Internet was just being introduced, and it was announced on the flight, but after a little bit it stopped working and they announced 'technical difficulties' and it wouldn't be available.

The guy next to him was like "this is bullshit": how quickly the world owed this guy something that he knew existed only a few minutes before.

As he goes on: often whenever people complain about their flights, it was like a 1940s German cattle car: X happened, then Y happened. And his response is: And then what happened? Did you fly in the air? Did you sit on a chair in the sky? Like a bird, like humans have been imaging since the tail of Icarus (and before)?

Hedonic adaptation is real (which is "fine" as far as it goes, as striving for better isn't a bad thing):

* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hedonic_treadmill

But given you're invoking history, it's easy how it is to forget the woe that humans lived in just a few decades before Apollo 8, and the incredible strides that happened (and that many people on the planet, even now, have yet to fully experience):

* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Rise_and_Fall_of_American_...

palata 2026/04/03 08:10
因此他们对 Artemis I 进行了第一次真正的测试，由于隔热罩的原因，它被认为是不安全的。因此他们修改了隔热罩，并没有费心对其进行真正的测试。我想，“快速行动，打破常规”？

当然，他们在地面上进行了测试。但这就是他们为阿尔忒弥斯一号所做的，我们知道那是多么成功。

So they made a first real test with Artemis I, and it was deemed unsafe because of the heat shield. So they modified the heat shield and didn't bother making a real test with it. "Move fast and break things", I guess?

Sure, they tested it on the ground. But that's what they did for Artemis I, and we know how successful that was.

desertrider12 2026/04/03 14:41
根据流传甚广的文章[0]，NASA 在获得阿耳忒弥斯 2 号重返大气层的数据后，并未对阿耳忒弥斯 2 号的隔热罩进行任何改动。 NASA 确实改变了 2 的轨道，他们使化合物“渗透性降低”，但这种改变是在 1 飞行之前进行的。

[0] <https://idlewords.com/2026/03/artemis_ii_is_not_safe_to_fly....>

According to the article [0] that's been making the rounds, NASA didn't make any changes to Artemis 2's heat shield after getting data from 1's re-entry. NASA did change the trajectory for 2, and they made the compound "less permeable" but that change was made before 1 flew.

在 HN 中查看
palata 2026/04/03 07:21
我从小就欣赏阿波罗任务等。

如今，我认识到这是一项繁重的工程，但我对我们在我们已经知道可以做到的事情上投入如此多的资源这一事实印象不深。事实上，人类已经在太空中生存了几十年。花费很多，但带来的却不多。

但更重要的是：我们还有更重要的问题需要解决，首先 我们的生存。当然，对于科学来说，将机器人发送到火星很有趣。送人去火星是没有用的。希望成为“星际物种”是荒谬的。认为火星“只是下一步，但我们会走得更远”绝对是疯狂的。

地球上的生命正在逐渐消亡（我们目前所生活的大规模灭绝发生的速度比恐龙灭绝的速度快几个数量级）。我们面临着巨大的能源问题，以及越来越多的全球不稳定因素。

当然，看着四个人快乐地乘坐一艘宇宙飞船前往月球 不需要他们 很有趣，就像看超级碗一样。就像超级碗一样，有很多忠实粉丝认为这是一年中最重要的赛事。然而，大多数人并不关心。我们已经不再是 1969 年了，现在的问题只是浪费足够的钱让一些人享受他们的生活。

I grew up admiring the Apollo mission and the likes.

Nowadays, I recognise that it is heavy engineering, but I am not so impressed by the fact that we are throwing so much resources at something that we already know we can do. In fact, we have had humans surviving in space for decades now. It's costing a lot, it's not bringing much.

But more than that: we have much more important problems to solve, starting with our survival. Sure, sending robots to Mars is interesting, for science. Sending people to Mars is useless. Hoping to become an "interplanetary species" is preposterous. Thinking that Mars is "just a next step, but we'll go further" is absolutely insane.

Life is literally, measurably dying on Earth (the current mass extinction we are living in is happening orders of magnitude faster than the one that killed the dinosaurs). We have a huge energy problem, and more and more global instability.

Sure, watching four humans happily travelling to the Moon in a spaceship that literally does not need them is fun, like watching the Superbowl. And like for the Superbowl, there are big fans for whom it is the most important event of the year. However, most people don't care. We're not in 1969 anymore, now it's just a matter of wasting enough money for some people to have the time of their life.

在 HN 中查看
Gagarin1917 2026/04/03 08:59
我不确定我是否在关注。大多数人不关心环境吗？因为事实肯定不是这样的。大多数人也不关心环境。

另外，你除了世界状况就没有其他兴趣吗？对娱乐、体育或科技新闻根本不感兴趣？我怀疑您是否使用 HN。

我敢打赌，即使世界客观上比以往任何时候都更好，你也不会关心。你只是在为自己的不在乎找借口。如果你不在乎也没关系，但这肯定不是因为世界现状。否则你根本不会有任何兴趣，包括 HackerNews。

I’m not sure I’m following. Do most people not care because of the environment? Because that’s certainly not the case. Most people don’t care about the environment either.

Plus, do you not have any other interests besides the state of the world? No interest in entertainment or sports or tech news at all? I doubt that if you’re on HN.

My bet is that you wouldn’t care even if the world was objectively better than ever. You’re just coming up with excuses for why you don’t care. It’s fine if you don’t care, but it’s certainly not because of the state of the world. Otherwise you wouldn’t have any interests at all, including HackerNews.

在 HN 中查看
outworlder 2026/04/03 07:49
> 这只是浪费足够的钱让一些人享受一生的时光而已。

这是一个愤世嫉俗的观点。我们这样做并不是为了让宇航员玩得开心。

是的，我们一直在搞砸我们的星球。仅就这一点而言，我们应该发展获取地球以外资源的能力。在我们有能力发射卫星之前，我们本可以提出同样的论点（“为什么我们要浪费资源向太空发送一些只能在人们快要饿死的时候发出蜂鸣声的东西？”）。如今，如果我们想有机会拯救地球上仅存的资源，它们就至关重要。

月球任务可能不会立即带来好处，但我们始终受益于太空任务的科学技术进步。我怀疑这次会有所不同。

与新的航空母舰相比，我当然更喜欢无数次的月球任务。

> it's just a matter of wasting enough money for some people to have the time of their life.

That's such a cynical viewpoint. We are not doing this so that astronauts can have fun.

Yes, we have been screwing up our planet. On that note alone, we should develop capabilities to access resources beyond our planet. We could have made that same argument before we had the capability of launching satellites ("why are we wasting resources sending something to space that can only beep while people are dying of hunger?"). Nowadays, they are crucial if we want to have a chance at saving what remains of our planet.

Moon missions may not give an immediate benefit, but we have always benefitted from scientific and technological advancements from space missions. I doubt it's going to be different this time.

I'd certainly prefer countless more moon missions than a new aircraft carrier.

在 HN 中查看
palata 2026/04/03 08:04
> 这是一个愤世嫉俗的观点。我们这样做并不是为了让宇航员玩得开心。

不要误会我的意思：我非常愿意站在他们的立场上，我完全理解为什么 他们 想要这样做。

> 如今，它们至关重要

这是典型的“我们需要这样做，因为这很困难，而且我们不知道从中能学到什么，顺便说一句，我们为太空计划开发的一些东西已经进入民用”的论点。

但它是有缺陷的。其一，我们今天比 50 年代知道的更多。这就像说“过去，他们认为地球是平的，所以谁知道呢，也许明天我们就会意识到人类有心灵遥感的能力”。事实是……“很可能不是”。

> 我们始终受益于太空任务的科学技术进步。我怀疑这次会有所不同。

让我们玩一个游戏：你不被允许阅读它。不一而足，不同的太空计划带来了哪些技术进步？双子座？阿波罗？联盟号？航天飞机？米尔？国际空间站？如果你设法给出多个正确答案，你是否真的相信如果没有相应的太空计划就不可能开发这项技术？我对此表示怀疑。

这就像说我们需要花费数十亿美元开发一辆赛车才能提高滑板的稳定性一样。从技术上讲，这是错误的，所以我听到的捍卫这个想法的唯一论据是“因为聪明的人会对开发赛车感兴趣，但如果不可能，他们将成为官僚或金融家，而不是改进滑板”。不太有说服力。

> 比起新的航空母舰，我当然更喜欢无数次的月球任务。

同意。但这并不是花费数十亿美元将人类送入太空的理由 为了自己的快乐 （而且并非没有风险）并为了所有喜欢从事这项工作的书呆子的乐趣（我也将自己视为这些书呆子的一部分）。

> That's such a cynical viewpoint. We are not doing this so that astronauts can have fun.

Don't get me wrong: I would totally love to be in their shoes, I completely understand why they want to do it.

> Nowadays, they are crucial

This is the typical "we need to do it because it's hard, and we don't know what we will learn from it, and BTW there are things we developed for the space program that got into civilian use" argument.

But it is flawed. For one, we know a lot more today than we did in the 50s. It would be like saying "in the past, they thought that the Earth was flat, so who knows, maybe tomorrow we will realise that humans are capable of telekinesis". The truth is... "most likely not".

> we have always benefitted from scientific and technological advancements from space missions. I doubt it's going to be different this time.

Let's play a game: you're not allowed to read about it. Off the top of your head, what technological advancements did the different space programs bring? Gemini? Apollo? Soyuz? The space shuttle? Mir? The ISS? And if you manage to give more than one correct answer to that, do you genuinely believe that it wouldn't have been possible to develop that technology without the corresponding space program? I doubt it.

It's like saying that we needed to spend billions developing a race car in order to improve the stability of a skateboard. Technically, that is wrong, so the only argument I heard to defend the idea was something like "because brilliant people would be interested in developing a race car, but if it wasn't possible, instead of improving skateboards, they would be bureaucrats or financiers". Not very convincing.

> I'd certainly prefer countless more moon missions than a new aircraft carrier.

Agreed. But that's not a justification for spending billions sending humans in space for their own pleasure (and not without risk) and for the pleasure of all the nerds who enjoy working on that (and I count myself as part of those nerds).

在 HN 中查看
fy20 2026/04/03 12:12
阿波罗计划对集成电路的发展产生了巨大影响，将软件工程转变为一门真正的学科，并对电传操纵技术产生了重大影响。如果没有的话这会发生吗？也许是的，这些技术已经在某种程度上存在了，但该计划比其他情况下更努力地推动它们。对于后来的太空任务也是如此，他们将技术推向了当时的极限。

太阳能电池板就是一个很好的例子。它们是在太空竞赛之前发明的，但是为什么在地球上需要它们呢？我们有廉价的石油和化石燃料，没有人关心可再生能源。但在它们发明后的前 50 年里，卫星是它们生存的动力，因为在卫星上使用这项技术是有意义的。这给了他们一个真正的用例，从而持续对它们进行投资和开发。

我怀疑如果没有太空竞赛，我们今天是否会拥有相同水平的卫星技术，因此我们也不太可能拥有相同水平的太阳能电池板。

The Apollo program had a big impact on the development of integrated circuits, turning software engineering into a real discipline, and fly-by-wire technology. Could this have happened without? Probably yes, these technologies aready somewhat existed, but the program pushed them much harder than they would have done otherwise. Same thing for later space missions, they pushed technology to the limits of the time.

A good example here is solar panels. They were invented before the space race, but for what, why do you need them on earth? We had cheap oil and fossil fuels, nobody cared about renewables. But for the first 50 years after they were invented satellites was what kept them alive, as it made sense to use that technology there. That gave them a real use case, which continued investment and development into them.

I doubt today we would have the same level of satellite technology today if the space race didn't happen, so it's unlikely we would have the same level of solar panels either.

在 HN 中查看
MichaelRo 2026/04/03 12:47
虽然我同意向月球发送载人任务有点无用的观点，但不幸的是，将这些钱用于“崇高目的”是一个乌托邦，因为事情不是这样的。

实际上，如果这数十亿美元不资助 NASA 项目，它们就会让一些亿万富翁变得更富有，Oracle 会解雇 30,000 人来资助数据中心，而这些数据中心在准备好时就会过时，或者类似的东西。没有一毛钱实现人类的崇高目标。

While I agree with the sentiment that sending manned missions to the Moon is kinda useless, unfortunately diverting those money to "noble purposes" is an utopia because that's not how things work.

In practice if those billions don't fund NASA programs they go into making some billionaires richer, Oracle laying off 30,000 people to fund data centers that will be obsolete by the time they are ready and similar stuff. Not a dime towards noble goals of humanity.

在 HN 中查看
majkinetor 2026/04/03 08:10
> 我们在我们已经知道可以做到的事情上投入了如此多的资源。

不，“我们”知道如何用 10 倍以上的资金和董事会成员以非常不安全的方式做到这一点。这是几次肌肉收缩，这就是它停止的原因。

让整个事情变得常规、廉价和安全是另一回事，而“我们”还不知道如何做到这一点，否则我们至少会有很少的科学家一直在月球上。

跑马拉松和死掉和一直跑马拉松是有区别的。

> 我们有...[其他]...问题

这种想法是无稽之谈。周围有这么多人，可以有任意一组人在解决任何类型的问题，而不需要他们指出其他组在解决想象中的问题。你说，除非每个人都致力于解决特定问题，否则它就不会得到解决。生活根本不是这样的，神话般的人月很好地解释了为什么，然后，你无法知道未探索的空间会带来什么（也许改变游戏规则的发现）。

> we are throwing so much resources at something that we already know we can do.

No, "we" knew how to do it with 10x more money and people on the board, in a very unsafely manner. It was a few times muscle flex and thats why it stopped.

Making entire thing routine, cheap and safe is something else, and "we" don't know yet how to do that, or we would have at least few scientists constantly on the Moon.

It's a difference between running a marathon and dropping dead, and doing it all the time.

> we have ...[other]... problems

This kind of thinking is nonsensical. With so many people around, there can be arbitrary group of people working on any kind of problem, without them needing to point to other groups as doing imaginary problems. You talk like unless everybody works on solving specific problem, its not going to get solved. Life simply doesn't work that way, mythical man month explained it well why for one, and then, you can't know what unexplored spaces bring (maybe game changing discoveries).

在 HN 中查看
palata 2026/04/03 08:29
我不确定你想说什么。所以人们应该对此感到欣喜若狂，因为“这几乎是同样的事情，但这次在船上玩得开心的人不会冒那么大的风险（除了美国宇航局知道隔热罩不安全而派他们去），而且整个事情便宜得多”？那么我们是否应该投资数十亿美元在 3 天而不是 6 天内去那里，并期望人们会给人留下深刻的印象？

> 周围有这么多人，可以有任意一群人在解决任何类型的问题

当然。只是这群特定的人用纳税人的钱来做这件事，而且它的用处显然不大。这笔钱可以用来……我不知道……养活人们？仅举一个例子。

> 你说除非每个人都致力于解决特定问题，否则它就不会得到解决。

事实上，如果你读过一些我提到的问题（即我们的生存），我认为相对清楚的是，“正确地解决它”是不可能的（那艘船已经航行了），而“糟糕地解决它”将需要付出相当多的牺牲。 每个人都活着。我们确实需要 大家 改变他们的生活方式 有更多的生存机会。即使这样也无法阻止大多数人发生非常糟糕的事情。

我的意思是对此非常乐观。捷径就是“我们完蛋了”。如果你没有意识到这一点，那可能是因为你没有真正理解这个问题。

I am not sure what you are trying to say. So people should be ecstatic about it because "it's almost the same thing, but this time the people having fun onboard are not taking remotely as much risk (other than NASA sending them knowing that the heat shield is unsafe), and the whole thing is a lot cheaper"? And then should we invest billions do go there in 3 days instead of 6, and expect that people will be impressed?

> With so many people around, there can be arbitrary group of people working on any kind of problem

Sure. It's just that this particular group of people does it with taxpayer money, and it's measurably not very useful. That money could go to... I don't know... feed people? Just one example.

> You talk like unless everybody works on solving specific problem, its not going to get solved.

Actually, if you read a bit about the problem that I am mentioning (i.e. our survival), I think it's relatively clear that "solving it properly" is impossible (that ship has sailed), and "solving it badly" will require sacrifices from pretty much everybody alive. We literally need everybody to change their lifestyle in order to have more chances of survival. And even that will not prevent very bad things from happening to most people.

And I am saying that being pretty optimistic about it. A shortcut is simply "we're pretty much screwed". And if you don't realise it, it's probably because you don't really understand the problem.

> 我们目前所生活的大规模灭绝发生的速度比杀死恐龙的那次要快几个数量级

迷人。我对灭绝事件的天真的看法是，它相对突然，在个人时间尺度上而不是地质时间尺度上——几十年甚至几代人。但看起来可能是“_快速灭绝，也许在不到一万年的时间内_”[0]。去展示地质和进化时间线是多么不直观！

[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_e...

> the current mass extinction we are living in is happening orders of magnitude faster than the one that killed the dinosaurs

Fascinating. My naive perception of the extinction event was that it was relatively sudden, on a personal rather than geological timescale - decades or maybe generations. But it looks like it might be "_rapid extinction, perhaps over a period of less than 10,000 years_" [0]. Goes to show how unintuitive geological and evolutionary timelines are!

[0] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_e...

在 HN 中查看
orbital-decay 2026/04/03 13:11
真正的答案是，单次发射月球任务并不那么有趣。如果你读过任何参与阿波罗计划的人（工程师或像吉恩·克兰兹这样的任务控制人员），每个人都知道让该计划继续下去的唯一因素就是新颖性。它已经不在那里了。我想说，与登月飞行相比，喷气推进实验室被低估了，因为这是我们对太阳系了解如此之多的主要原因。

此外，阿波罗终止后没有太多技术被重用，这个项目非常相似。最初的苏联计划（轨道组装和金星/火星飞越）和 SpaceX 的计划要务实得多，因为它们带来了更多实用技术和基础设施，即使没有完全实现（轨道站和重型可重复使用发射器）。

The real answer is that one of a kind single-launch Moon mission is not all that interesting. If you read anyone who participated in the Apollo program (engineers or mission control people like Gene Kranz), everyone knew that the only thing that kept the program afloat was novelty. It's not there anymore. I would say JPL is way too underrated compared to the moon flights, as it's the main reason we know so much about the Solar System.

Also, not a lot of tech from Apollo was reused after its termination, and this program is very similar. Original Soviet plan (orbital assembly and Venus/Mars flyby) and SpaceX's one much later were/are a lot more pragmatic, as they led to a lot more practical tech and infra even if not fully realized (orbital stations and heavy reusable launchers).

starkparker 2026/04/03 12:51
1968 年 12 月阿波罗 8 号升空时，美国正处于卷入昂贵且计划不周的越南战争的高峰期，美国的通货膨胀已经持续三年，并在 10 月份达到 7.2%，注定失败、腐败且偏执的共和党当选总统在竞选中降低了汉堡包和医疗保健的价格，但在幕后积极试图操纵最高法院的组成，以色列正在轰炸其邻国，对巴拿马运河的控制正在被削弱。争议不断，香港流感疫情逐渐平息，涉及名人的绑架故事成为热门新闻，国家运输安全委员会 (NTSB) 正在努力解释为何如此多的客机发生致命坠机事故以及与地面结构相撞的事件
When Apollo 8 went up in December 1968, the US was near the peak of its involvement in the expensive and poorly planned Vietnam war, inflation had been rising in the US for three years and had hit 7.2% in October, the doomed, corrupt, and paranoid Republican president-elect had ran on lowering the price of hamburgers and medical care but was actively trying to manipulate the Supreme Court's composition behind the scenes, Israel was bombing its neighbors, control over the Panama Canal was being contested, the Hong Kong Flu epidemic was winding down, popular news was gripped by a kidnapping story involving a celebrity, and the NTSB was struggling to explain why so many passenger aircraft were being involved in fatal crashes and collisions with ground structures

zabzonk 2026/04/03 10:33
> 没人知道名字

我不用谷歌就知道——迈克·柯林斯。我还知道第三个在上面行走的人的名字 - 皮特·康拉德 (Pete Conrad)，以及他离开 LEM 时所说的话（也许不准确）：“这对尼尔来说可能是一小步，但对我来说却是一大步”

我现在可以拿到奖品了吗？？

> nobody knows the name

I didn't have to google for it to know - Mike Collins. I also knew the the name of the third guy to walk on it - Pete Conrad, and what he said getting out of the LEM (perhaps not precisely): "That may be a small step for Neil, but it was a big one for me"

Can I have my prize now??

Gagarin1917 2026/04/03 07:55
大多数人并不关心阿波罗11号时期。

他们知道这件事发生了，但他们对它或历史的兴趣为零。

这就是为什么现在普通人不关心的原因。他们实际上从未这样做过。

Most people don’t care about Apollo 11 period.

They know it happened but they have zero interest in it or the history.

That’s why the average person doesn’t cares now. They never actually did.

在 HN 中查看
palata 2026/04/03 08:18
好吧，这种比较并不是人们对阿波罗 11 号的看法 现在，但是他们的想法 那时。

当时，这是一件轰动全球新闻的大事件。

阿耳忒弥斯二号昨天发射，我的非工程师亲戚和朋友甚至不知道它发生了（他们甚至不知道这是计划好的）。

Well the comparison is not what people think about Apollo 11 right now, but what they thought back then.

Back then, it was a big event that made the news worldwide.

Artemis II launched yesterday, and my non-engineer relatives and friends don't even know it happened (they don't even know it was planned).

在 HN 中查看
Gagarin1917 2026/04/03 08:22
我向你保证，这是全球新闻。各大新闻机构和地方新闻频道都对此进行了谈论。

不过，人们不再从新闻机构获取“新闻”。他们从社交媒体算法中获得信息，如果他们事先对任何太空或与太空无关的事物没有兴趣，他们就不会得到有关它的新闻。

即使他们确实听说过，也可能没有任何联系，他们的大脑会在“城市公交车成功停靠在公交车站”的同一个地方填满它。因为他们根本不在乎。

无论好坏，文化远没有那么集中。

It WAS global news, I assure you. Every major news agency and local news channels talked about it.

People don’t get their “news” from news agencies anymore, though. They get it from their social media algorithms, and if they have no prior interest in anything space or tangential to space, they won’t get news about it.

And if they did hear about it, it probably didn’t connect whatsoever, and their brain filled it away in the same place as “city bus makes successful stop at bus stop.” Because they couldn’t care less.

Culture is far less centralized, for better or worse.

在 HN 中查看
dingaling 2026/04/03 14:26

他们对太空飞行不感兴趣，但他们仍然得到了消息

I disagree, I work with a bunch of Gen Zs who live in TikTok and talk in meme-slang, and several of them stayed up late to watch the launch.

They're not anyway interested in spaceflight but they still got the news

breve 2026/04/03 07:45
> 阿波罗计划是美国一个世纪技术变革的三重感叹号，在此期间，美国人实现了城市电气化，街道上充满了汽车，天空中充满了飞机，分裂了原子，并发明了数字计算机。

看看现在的美国。反复无常、好战、随心所欲地征收关税、威胁吞并加拿大和格陵兰岛、威胁退出北约、疏远盟友。

不要低估美国已经和仍在对自己造成的声誉损害。

> The Apollo program was the triple-back-handspring exclamation mark on a century of American technological transformations, during which Americans had electrified their cities, filled their streets with cars and their skies with airplanes, split atoms, and invented digital computers.

And look at America now. Erratic, belligerent, applying tariffs on a whim, threatening to annex Canada and Greenland, threatening to leave NATO, alienating itself from allies.

Don't underestimate the reputational damage America has done and is still doing to itself.

在 HN 中查看
Gagarin1917 2026/04/03 07:55
在阿波罗任务期间，美国在越南犯下了融化婴儿等战争罪行。

世界各地的年轻人被征召入伍，被带离家乡，被迫杀人。

非裔美国人正在为基本权利和平等而奋斗。

一位总统、一位主要总统候选人和最著名的民权领袖都被暗杀。

这不像阿波罗计划发生在美国的黄金时代之类的……

如果你确实欣赏阿波罗，那么你没有理由不欣赏阿尔忒弥斯。

During the Apollo missions, the US was melting babies in Vietnam, amongst other war crimes.

Young men were being drafted, taken from home, and forced to kill people across the world.

African Americans were fighting for basic rights and equality.

A President, a major Presidential candidate, and the most prominent civil rights leader were all assassinated.

It’s not like Apollo was happening during the golden age of America or something…

If you actually do appreciate Apollo, there’s no reason you shouldn’t be able to appreciate Artemis.

autoexec 2026/04/03 07:27
“重返月球”听起来很欺骗，因为我们实际上并没有去月球，我们只是发射一枚火箭绕月球飞行。真正的登月将会受到更多关注。这次发射对我来说更令人印象深刻的是，这将是人类进入太空最远的一次。我认为美国宇航局公关团队如果把这个作为标题，而不是所有这些“登上月球！”会做得更好。讲话
"Back to the moon" sounds deceptive since we're not actually going to the moon, we're just sending a rocket around it. An actual moon landing will get a lot more attention. What's far more impressive about this launch to me is that it will be the farthest out into space people have been. I think the NASA PR team would have done better making that the headline rather than all this "to the moon!" talk

在 HN 中查看
joelshep 2026/04/03 13:13
几乎没有。回想起 1968 年底，阿波罗 8 号首次载人离开地球轨道并绕月飞行：

新闻记者沃尔特·克朗凯特 (Walter Cronkite) 回忆阿波罗 8 号那一年：“整个 20 世纪 60 年代在 1968 年达到顶峰，毫无疑问，这是 20 世纪最可怕的十年，很可能是我们整个历史，甚至包括内战的十年。美国陷入了自内战、越南战争和民权斗争以来从未有过的分裂。

“一切似乎都在 68 年达到了紧要关头。两位更自由主义事业的领导人被暗杀。鲍比·肯尼迪 (Bobby Kennedy) 在加利福尼亚州赢得选举后不久，这可能会让他成为当年的总统候选人，而马丁·路德·金 (Martin Luther King) 在孟菲斯当然是对整个民权事业的沉重打击。到了 68 年夏天，民主党全国代表大会变成了芝加哥人暴力和反暴力的可怕混乱。警察……到了十二月，这个国家已经陷入了低谷。”

Hardly. Remembering the end of 1968, when Apollo 8 made the first manned voyage out of earth's orbit, and orbited the moon:

Newsman Walter Cronkite remembers the year of Apollo 8: "The whole 1960s really culminating in 1968 were the most terrible decade, undoubtedly, of the twentieth century and very possibly our entire history, even including the decade of the Civil War. America was divided as it never had been since the Civil War and by the Vietnam War, by the civil rights fight.

"Everything seemed to come to a head in '68. There were the assassinations of two of the leaders of the more liberal causes. Bobby Kennedy, shortly after winning that election in California that probably would have put him over the top as the presidential candidate that year, and Martin Luther King, of course, in Memphis, was a terrible blow to the entire cause of civil rights. By the summer of '68 the Democratic convention turned out to be a terrible shambles of violence and counter-violence by the Chicago police... By December the country was pretty far down."

PaulRobinson 2026/04/03 07:19
13 年内投入 930 亿美元对于一个已经开始围绕个人自我进行调整的项目来说并不算什么，而当时美国大多数人都在努力维持收支平衡。

不幸的是，我认为阿耳忒弥斯将在今年年底被取消。如果隔热罩不能像一些观察家担心/警告的那样持续下去，也许到四月底。

我希望我错了。

$93 billion over 13 years doesn't feel like a great deal for a program that has started to align around a single person's ego, when most of the US is struggling to make ends meet.

I think Artemis will be cancelled by the end of the year, unfortunately. If the heat shield doesn't hold up as some observers fear/have warned, perhaps by the end of April.

I hope I'm wrong.

outworlder 2026/04/03 07:56

伊朗入侵仅一个月后，我们的成本就达到了阿耳忒弥斯总成本的一半左右。这场战争的一周可以为阿耳忒弥斯计划一年提供资金。你认为这样更划算吗？

gedy 2026/04/03 07:29
仅用于新冠疫情刺激措施，我们就发放了 3000 亿美元现金，这并不算疯狂，特别是如果你考虑到投入使用和保留的知识和技能。

在 HN 中查看
g947o 2026/04/03 07:52
一是真正的现金进入普通人手中用于日常购买，这已被证明（在各种研究中）对父母/家庭和经济困难的人有所帮助。

另一种是“知识和技能”，这些知识和技能似乎与人们的生活遥不可及。

作为一个生活不受这两者影响太大的人，我每次都会选择刺激。

One is real cash going into the hands of ordinary people for everyday purchases, which has proven (in various studies) to have helped parents/families and the financially struggled.

The other is "knowledge and skills" that seem remote and detached from people's lives.

As someone whose life isn't affected much by either of these, I would choose the stimulus every time.

在 HN 中查看
Gagarin1917 2026/04/03 07:47
基本事实是，绝大多数人都对太空和太空探索毫不在意。

它太抽象、太复杂、太遥远，让他们感觉不到与之有联系。它不再与民族自豪感挂钩，与悲剧无关（通常），与他们认为自己认识的名人无关（凯蒂·佩里没有参与这次发布）……普通人没有任何联系。

所有其他解释都只是那些觉得自己应该关心但从未关心过的人的借口。

The basic truth is, the vast majority of people couldn’t give less of a fuck about space and space exploration.

It’s just too abstract, too complicated, and too far away for them to feel connected to it. It’s not attached to national pride (anymore), it’s not connected to tragedy (typically), it’s not connected to celebrities they feel like they know (Katy Perry isn’t involved with this launch)… there’s just nothing for the average person to connect with.

Every other explanation is just an excuse from people who feel like they should care, but never have.

我可能会被否决，但有两件事： 首先，我觉得很好，我们再次派人去那里。最后。我的意思是，我们在 60 年代就这样做过，但我想我们正在恢复，这很酷。

其次，我发现我们还没有尝试过。

我现在真的很难去关心。我很高兴看到令人兴奋的新发展和持续的努力。

I’m probably going to get downvoted, but two things: Firstly, I thought ok great we’re sending people there again. Finally. I mean we did this in the 60s but cool that we’re resuming I guess.

Secondly I found out we aren’t even try that yet.

It’s really difficult for me to care at this point. I would love to see exciting new developments and sustained efforts.

在 HN 中查看
metalman 2026/04/03 07:13
这至少是语义上的错位，因为它们肯定不会“去月球”，而是会以巨大的速度进行巨大的摆动，因为它们没有燃料来燃烧成圆形LLO（低月球轨道），然后回家，实际上只是弹弓回到地球。 不过祝他们好运。
it is at a minimum, semantic missalignment, as they are most defintly not going " to the moon", but are going to take a huge swing by at a huge speed, because they dont have the fuel to do a burn into a circular LLO (low lunar orbit),and then get home, and are realy just slingshoting back to earth. wish them luck in that though.

TimorousBestie 2026/04/03 07:28
如今，即使是“真实新闻”也乏味且写得不好。例如，《纽约时报》发射前的报道几乎没有传达任务参数。

彼得·贝克今天的文章（ https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/us/politics/artemis-ii-la... ）更多的是政治胡言乱语，对科学目标也很少，只是象征性地提到了后续任务。

Even “real news” these days is insipid and poorly written. For instance, the NYT coverage pre-launch barely communicated the mission parameters.

Today’s article by Peter Baker ( https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/us/politics/artemis-ii-la... ) was yet more political drivel and very light on scientific goals, just a token mention of follow-on missions.

trhway 2026/04/03 07:24
我们将乘坐星际飞船返回月球并前往火星，这只是 SpaceX 的正常路线图。这让我对太空的未来感到兴奋——它只是作为一项业务的常态，一项盈利良好的业务。阿耳忒弥斯的存在与否不会对我们的太空未来产生太大影响。

阿耳忒弥斯计划和硬件是一项巨大的政府拨款计划，即使该计划进入着陆阶段，它仍然是不可持续的一次性计划，其着陆次数可能比阿波罗计划还要少。

建立月球基地、商业旅行和那里的开发——这一切都是星际飞船（自然地前提是 SpaceX 成功地使其每次发射达到 5-1000 万美元——如果不是 SpaceX，其他人无论如何也会这么做）

正如我几天前所写，Artemis/SLS 永远无法达到商业水平 https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47583438

we're going back to Moon and going to Mars too on Starship, and it is just a normal roadmap of the SpaceX. And that makes me excited about space future - normalcy of it just being a business, a good profitable business. Where is existence or not of Artemis wouldn't change much our space future.

Artemis program and hardware is a huge government money appropriation program, and even if the program makes it to the landing phase, it would still be an unsustainable one-off with probably even less landings than the Apollo program.

Establishing of Moon bases, commercial travel and development there - it is all Starship (naturally predicated on SpaceX success at getting it to $5-10M/launch - if not SpaceX, somebody else would anyway do it)

As i wrote couple days ago the Artemis/SLS will never be able to get to that commercial level https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47583438

charlie90 2026/04/03 10:27
大约在同一时期，中国斥资 300B 美元建造了高铁。阿耳忒弥斯的成本是其 1/3。有许多令人印象深刻的工程可以做到，这对普通人来说也很酷且有用。去月球只是虚荣。
China built out their high speed rail for $300B over about the same period. Artemis is 1/3rd the cost of that. Theres lots of impressive engineering to do thats also cool and useful to ordinary people. Going to the moon is just vanity.
